data.title: KazMunayGas resumes oil exports via Baku-Tbilisi-Ceyhan pipeline
data.description:
Kazakhstan has resumed oil exports via the Baku-Tbilisi-Ceyhan (BTC) pipeline system, the press service of KazMunayGas (KMG) said, as per Interfax.

"On September 13, 8,800 tonnes of Kashagan oil were shipped from the port of Aktau in the direction of the Baku-Tbilisi-Ceyhan pipeline. The next shipment is planned for September 20," KMG said.

Supplies of Kazakh oil via this route were temporarily suspended in August 2025. Prior to this, in July, a number of media outlets citing sources reported that excessive content of organic chlorides (substances used to increase production) had been detected in certain batches of Azerbaijani Azeri Light oil.

On July 24, BP in Azerbaijan reported that quality checks of oil at all facilities along the BTC confirmed that it met specification requirements. Organic chlorides were found in some tanks at the terminal in the Turkish port of Ceyhan.

KMG and the State Oil Company of the Azerbaijani Republic (SOCAR) signed an agreement on the transit of Kazakh oil in 2022. In 2024, the parties concluded a new agreement providing for a phased increase in volumes pumped through Azerbaijani territory.

The Baku-Tbilisi-Ceyhan pipeline, with a length of around 1,700 km, connects the Caspian region with the Turkish Mediterranean port of Ceyhan. Its throughput capacity reaches 50 million tonnes of oil per year. For Kazakhstan, the BTC plays a strategic role in diversifying exports and ensuring stable access to global markets.

data.title: Greece's current account surplus widens in July, tourism surges
data.description:
Greece recorded a current account surplus of 938 million euros ($1.1 billion) in July, up from a surplus of 210 million euros in the same month a year earlier, due to robust tourism revenue, data from the Bank of Greece showed on Friday.

Receipts from tourism, a key economic driver for the Mediterranean country, surged by an annual 15% in July to 4.5 billion euros, with foreign arrivals rising by 6.4%, the central bank said.

This has more than offset a deficit in the balance of goods, a result of imports outweighing exports.

($1 = 0.8500 euros)

data.title: EU to consult IMF on size of EU Reparations Loan to Ukraine
data.description:
Key points:
  • EU considers using frozen Russian assets for Ukraine loan
  • IMF assessment crucial for determining loan size
  • Belgium sceptical about proposal's risks and uncertainties

By Jacob Gronholt-Pedersen and Jan Strupczewski

The European Commission will include the International Monetary Fund's assessment of Ukraine's financing needs over the next two years in deciding how big the EU's Reparations Loan to Kyiv should be, EU Economic Commissioner Valdis Dombrovskis said.

The European Union is discussing ways to use frozen Russian assets to underpin a "reparation loan" to Ukraine to bolster its wartime finances as U.S. President Donald Trump is curbing direct U.S.-funded military aid to Kyiv.

"What the Commission is proposing is a reparations loan, so basically providing a loan to Ukraine by using cash balances of immobilized Russian assets without... touching Russia's claim on those assets," Dombrovskis told reporters on entering talks between EU finance ministers in Copenhagen.

WAITING FOR IMF ASSESSMENT ON FINANCING NEEDS

"We are right now working on all the modalities, on timings, on volumes. For volumes, it will be important for us also to see the IMF assessment on the finance needs for Ukraine over the next two years, and the IMF is currently finalising this work," he said.

Spanish Finance Minister Carlos Cuerpo said he expected a Commission proposal on the loan in October. "From the Spanish perspective, we will be supportive," Cuerpo told reporters.

There is around $300 billion in Russian central bank assets immobilised in G7 countries, the bulk of which is in Europe and in particular in Belgium, where the Euroclear securities repository holds 194 billion euros ($228.24 billion) of the assets.

Of the 194 billion euros in securities, some 170 billion have now become cash as the securities matured since their freezing when Moscow invaded Ukraine in February 2022. It is not clear how much of that money could be used for the Reparations Loan idea.

LOAN RISKS TO BE SHARED AMONG EU GOVERNMENTS

Dombrovskis said the model that the Commission was working on would be replicable in all G7 countries that hold some of the frozen Russian assets and that he discussed the idea with G7 finance ministers during a recent call.

The Commission has not disclosed any details of the reparations loan idea apart from the assumption that Ukraine would only repay the loan once it receives reparations from Russia for war damage and that risks associated with the loan would be shared by EU governments.

EU officials involved in the discussions of the idea said it would involve replacing the Russian assets held in Europe with zero-coupon bonds issued by the European Commission. The bonds would have guarantees from either all EU countries or just those willing to participate.

The government guarantees are the politically risky feature because they could be called upon if Russia makes claims once EU sanctions against Moscow are reversed.

Belgian Finance Minister Vincent Van Peteghem said he was sceptical about the idea.

He called the proposal "rather vague" and stressed risks need to be shared among all EU member states.

($1 = 0.8500 euros)

data.title: Canadian police seize $40 million in crypto from unregistered exchange TradeOgre
data.description:
Canada's national police service announced Thursday that it has seized 56 million Canadian dollars ($40 million) worth of crypto assets from local crypto exchange TradeOgre.

This is the largest cryptocurrency seizure in the country's history, according to the official statement from the Royal Canadian Mounted Police (RCMP). The Canadian police have also dismantled the exchange.

The country's Money Laundering Investigative Team launched a probe on the exchange in June 2024 following a tip from Europol, the statement said. The team discovered that TradeOgre was running an unregistered operation as a money services business and failed to identify its clients.

"Investigators have reason to believe that the majority of funds transacted on TradeOgre came from criminal sources," the statement said. "The main attraction of this type of platform, which doesn’t require users to identify themselves to make an account, is that it hides the source of funds."

The Canadian police haven't pressed charges yet on anyone associated with the trading platform, stating that the investigation is still ongoing.

TradeOgre's official website currently displays a banner from the RCMP that says — "This site and its cryptoassets have been seized by the RCMP." The exchange was last active on the social media platform X on May 28, 2025.

data.title: Iran's deputy foreign minister criticises European powers over sanctions plan
data.description:
By Olivia Le Poidevin

Iran on Friday criticised a move by European powers to impose international sanctions by the end of the month if Tehran does not meet conditions, including access for U.N. nuclear inspectors.

"What Europeans are doing is politically biased and politically motivated ... They are wrong on different levels by trying to misuse the mechanism embedded in the Joint Comprehensive Plan of Action (JCPOA)," Iranian Deputy Foreign Minister Saeed Khatibzadeh told reporters in Geneva.

European powers will likely reimpose international sanctions on Iran by the end of the month after their latest round of talks with Tehran aimed at preventing them were deemed not serious, France's President Emmanuel Macron said on Thursday.

Britain, France and Germany, the so-called E3, launched a 30-day process at the end of August to reimpose U.N. sanctions. They set conditions for Tehran to meet during September to convince them to delay the "snapback mechanism".

Iran's Foreign Minister Abbas Araqchi said later on Thursday that he had presented a "reasonable and actionable plan."

The offer by the E3 to put off the snapback for up to six months to enable serious negotiations is conditional on Iran restoring access for U.N. nuclear inspectors - who would also seek to account for Iran's large stock of enriched uranium - and engaging in talks with the U.S.

Khatibzadeh said all options are on the table if diplomacy fails.

"If Europeans go on this path, they are making the level of unpredictability to the highest level possible, and they are responsible for...any possible future risks," he said.

The U.N. Security Council will vote on Friday on a resolution that would permanently lift U.N. sanctions on Iran. It is likely to fail to get the minimum nine votes needed to pass, say diplomats, and if it did it would be vetoed by the United States, Britain or France.

data.title: Tech, Media & Telecom Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Technology, Media and Telecom. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0535 GMT - China's biggest bottleneck in reducing reliance on Nvidia chips is its limited chip manufacturing capability due to U.S. restrictions, say Jefferies analysts in a research note. A flurry of new local AI chips announcements recently have boosted market confidence in China's chip self-reliance. However, China's most advanced contract chip maker, SMIC, still faces constraints on producing 7-nanometer chips without advanced equipment, they say. Huawei's plan last year to roll out Ascend 910D using 5-nanometer technology hasn't materialized due to poor yield, the analysts say. Should U.S. and China reach a trade deal, there could be some potential easing of U.S. export restrictions on chip-making equipment, they add. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

0432 GMT - Nvidia's $5 billion investment puts Intel back at the center of the AI ecosystem, Saxo chief investment strategist Charu Chanana says in a research note. For Intel to co-develop CPUs that plug directly into Nvidia's GPUs, its chips move from a supporting role to a core driver of performance, the strategist notes. Meanwhile, the deal gives Intel fresh credibility with customers and "plants seeds for Nvidia to eventually use Intel's foundry as a second manufacturing source if TSMC capacity becomes strained or geopolitics intervene," she adds. For Nvidia, the partnership provides "flexibility and reach," as it strengthens Nvidia's grip on AI data centers and opens the door to new categories of AI PCs, she says. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

1822 GMT - Workday's core business continues to pose a large growth opportunity, D.A. Davidson analysts Gil Luria and Lucky Schreiner say in a note. The company's management sees a large addressable market of $188 billion for its core products, compared to $160 billion last year, given under penetrated markets with U.S. federal customers and frontline workers, the analysts say. The company is also working to simplify its platform to gain traction with mid-sized enterprises, which represent over half of the total available market, they say. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1818 GMT - Workday's new product announcements and financial disclosures put it in a better position to reap the benefits of growing agentic AI adoption across human resources, D.A. Davidson analysts Gil Luria and Lucky Schreiner say in a note. Workday is making it easier for customers to adopt new AI agents, and has been able to buy AI tools that can work off its curated, high quality enterprise data, the analysts say. It also requires agents to have a measured return on investment before release, they say. However, with some of its new AI agents set for release next year, further revenue growth will come mostly from acquisitions in the meantime, limiting upside to near-term numbers, they say. They raise their price target on stock to $260 from $225.Workday is down 0.6% to $233.57. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1808 GMT - Sangoma Technologies' return to growth is taking longer than expected, TD Cowen's David Kwan says in a report as the stock sinks 14% to C$7.27 after FY26 guidance came in soft. Sangoma's revenue outlook of C$200 million-C$210 million was 4% below expectations at midpoint, the analyst says. However, the stock may already have poor performance baked in. "We believe this is mostly reflected in the current discount valuation," the analyst says. Shares are down 27% year-to-date, which Kwan says makes the valuation "attractive, along with its [roughly] 15% free cash flow yield," as "execution and visibility are improving." (adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

1708 GMT - CrowdStrike's new solutions and features show it continues to innovate at an impressive pace, Junaid Siddiqui of Truist Securities says in a note. The company introduced seven new AI agents to automate security workflows at its recent conference, as well as a no-code platform for customers to build their own security agents, among other innovations, the analyst says. Those new solutions and features should help the company to benefit from a number of continued tailwinds, including vendor consolidation and a heightened threat environment, Siddiqui says, raising the price target to $550 from $500. CrowdStrike climbs 11% at $496.34.(kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1650 GMT - CrowdStrike's flexible subscription licensing model for its Falcon platform appears to be boosting the company's traction with both enterprise and small- and mid-sized businesses, JPMorgan analysts say in a note. Flex remained a central theme throughout the company's recent conference, both in conversations with customers and partners as well as during presentations, the analysts say. "Investor sessions and customer panels reinforced CrowdStrike's position as the 'oxygen' of cybersecurity, with Flex contracts driving deeper module adoption, larger deal sizes, and streamlined procurement," they say. CrowdStrike surges 11%.(kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1600 GMT — CrowdStrike remains the "gold standard" in cybersecurity, with its new AI products positioning it to lead the cybersecurity industry in agentic AI, Wedbush Securities analysts say in a note. That's especially important as organizations face increasingly complex cybersecurity threats, with a majority of organizations expecting a 20% increase in non-human interactions next year, the analysts say. "With the threat landscape now allowing conventionally underskilled adversaries to leverage AI and increase sophistication of malware, CRWD provides security teams necessary tools to combat these attacks by positioning its customers for the modern battlefield," they say. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1510 GMT - FCC Chair Brendan Carr tells CNBC that he thinks Jimmy Kimmel's comments about the political affiliation of Charlie Kirk's alleged assassin appeared to "directly mislead the American public." Carr's claim comes a day after Disney's ABC suspended "Jimmy Kimmel Live!" in response to Kimmel speculating that Tyler Robinson, the alleged killer of the far-right ideologist, was connected to Donald Trump's MAGA movement. Carr says on CNBC that the FCC's broadcast TV licenses come with "an obligation to operate in the public interest." Carr praised Nexstar Media Group and Sinclair, the owners of some ABC TV stations, for saying they would drop Kimmel's show over the comments. Nexstar is seeking approval for its acquisition of fellow broadcaster Tegna. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

1433 GMT - Meta showcased its new Meta Ray-Ban Display smart glasses yesterday and while the product is clearly improving, it's still a ways away from becoming mainstream and won't challenge Apple's wearable devices, says Oppenheimer analyst Martin Yang. The $800 price of the glasses is a step toward making them accessible, but their weight at 67 grams is still burdensome, Yang says. The technology required to reduce the weight is still far from meeting commercial pricing and volume. As a result, Meta's glasses won't yet pose a threat to Apple's ecosystem around iPhone, AirPods and Apple Watch, Yang says. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

1351 GMT - National Economic Council Director Kevin Hassett said on CNBC that he wasn't aware of anyone in the government negotiating with Nvidia to encourage it to invest in Intel. The U.S. announced it was taking a 10% stake in Intel last month and Hassett says Intel wasn't meeting the marks it needed to in order to receive funds from the CHIPS Act. "Instead of just throwing money away into a business that may or may not succeed, the U.S. government is helping the business succeed and making it so that the taxpayer benefits if they're successful at that," Hassett says. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

1326 GMT - Dutch supplier of chip-making equipment ASML Holding could benefit from a partnership between Nvidia and Intel to jointly develop data-center and personal-computing products, ING's Jan Frederik Slijkerman says. Intel is a longtime customer of ASML's lithography tools, which are essential to produce semiconductors. ASML shares jumped nearly 8% after Nvidia said it would inject $5 billion into Intel as part of their collaboration. Slijkerman says the increase in ASML's stock price shows investors expect more demand for lithography tools. ASML shares trade 7.3% higher at 790.80 euros. (mauro.orru@wsj.com)

data.title: Health Care Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering the Health Care sector. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0719 GMT - Medikaloka Hermina is poised to benefit from Astra International's doubling of its stake to 20%, Maybank Sekuritas Indonesia analysts say in a research report. This transaction supports the hospital-chain operator's long-term growth potential in Indonesia's private hospital sector and strengthens its alignment with a leading Indonesian conglomerate, the analysts say. Astra International's increased involvement is likely a mutually beneficial development. Synergies between the two companies and the success of Medikaloka Hermina's strategy to target the mid- to high-end market should serve as additional upside to its growth. The stock remains one of the brokerage's top picks in the sector, with a buy rating and a target price of IDR1,800.00. Shares are 0.6% lower at IDR1,675.00.(ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0357 GMT - ResMed's bull at Citi thinks the market is underestimating the breath-tech provider's medium-term margin-expansion capacity. Analyst Laura Sutcliffe points out that consensus for a fiscal 2026 gross margin of 62% is in-line with guidance, but ignores management's later growth ambitions. She thinks that 63% is achievable in fiscal 2027 and beyond. A good performance through fiscal 2026 could prompt analysts to rerate the stock, she writes in a note. Citi initiates coverage of ResMed's U.S.-listed stock with a US$330.00 target price and a buy rating. It also rated ResMed's Australia-listed stock a buy, and lifts its target price 4.1% to A$51.00. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

0319 GMT - Cochlear loses its bull at Citi, where analyst Laura Sutcliffe sees potential for affordability issues to weigh on consumer demand for its hearing tech. Sutcliffe tells clients in a note that she remains optimistic that the Australia-listed company can maintain its market-leading position, but suspects that fiscal 2026 growth will be weighted toward the second half of the period. She points out that while the knowledge that newer products were on the way meant some patients were reluctant to upgrade in fiscal 2025, affordability was also a factor in delayed spending. Citi cuts its target price by 8.6% to A$320.00 and reduces its recommendation to neutral from buy. Shares are down 1.0% at A$292.00. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

0137 GMT - Hartalega's earnings outlook appears stable even as uncertainties over the supply and demand of gloves linger heading into 2026, Hong Leong Investment Bank analyst Chee Kok Siang says in a note. The glove maker is decommissioning its less efficient Bestari Jaya facilities while upgrading production lines and automation capabilities, he says. These moves are expected to reinforce its position as Malaysia's most efficient producer of generic medical examination gloves, he notes. Hartalega's strong net cash of 986 million ringgit as of 1Q provides financial flexibility to weather industry challenges, he adds. Hong Leong maintains a hold rating on Hartalega and keeps its target price at MYR1.32. Shares are 0.8% lower at MYR1.19. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com) (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

0100 GMT - Pharmaceutical supplier Ebos shakes its bear at Citi following the recent sharp selloff in its stock. With the stock down by more than 25% since the dual-listed company reported its annual results on Aug. 27, the investment bank raises its recommendation to neutral from sell. Analyst Laura Sutcliffe says shares are now trading at about 17 times estimated FY 2027 core earnings, which is close to their historical average. She sets her target price a little lower to reflect the softer-than-expected margins reported last month. Citi cuts its target price on Ebos's Australia-listed stock by 12.5% to A$28.00. Shares are up 3.0% at A$26.15. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

2342 GMT - There doesn't appear to be a quick fix for Ramsay Health Care's Elysium mental health services business in the U.K., according to Citi. "Persistent occupancy problems and cost pressures lead us to think the road to a better performance is at least two years long," analyst Laura Sutcliffe says. Elysium's underlying Ebit fell 61% in FY 2025, compared to a 17% rise by its other U.K. operations. Earlier this year Ramsay paused investment in adding new sites for Elysium as it concentrates on improving performance. For Citi, Ramsay is effectively in damage limitation mode. "We do not rule out more drastic action from management," the bank says. It has a neutral call on Ramsay, and trims its price target by 10% to A$36.00/share. Ramsay ended Thursday at A$32.95. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

1321 GMT - Novo Nordisk has clear leadership in the field of orally-taken obesity treatments following trial data for its Wegovy weight-loss pill and a competing tablet from Eli Lilly, UBS analyst Matthew Weston writes. Lilly had already released headline data for its Orforglipron pill, delivering up to 12.4% weight-loss at the highest dose, below the 16.6% weight-loss in a trial of Novo Nordisk's pill, Weston adds. Further data at this week's EASD medical conference in Austria showed Orforglipron weight-loss plateaued relatively early and even saw weight regain at the 12 milligram dose, he adds. "Orforglipron still presents a threat on being a more scalable product, and therefore potentially discounted price." It also doesn't require 30 minutes fasting when taking it, so could be seen as more convenient, he says. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)Abhishek Raval is an analyst at AlphaValue. "Novo Nordisk's Prospects Could Turn Around — Market Talk," at 1200 GMT, misstated the firm's name.

1200 GMT - Novo Nordisk's efficacy edge in its new obesity pills on top of Wegovy's additional health benefits should stem the Danish pharmaceutical company's market-share erosion and turn around its prospects, AlphaValue's Abhishek Raval writes. New weight-loss candidate CagriSema's healthy commercial potential and swiftly acting new management should also help, he adds. The massive collapse in Novo Nordisk's share price over the past 15 months, due to competitive concerns, pricing pressure and a few other factors, seems largely overdone to AlphaValue. "The current valuation levels bake in excessive fears, providing a good opportunity for contrarian investors." Shares rise 7.2%. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com) Corrections & Amplifications

This article was corrected at 08:32 a.m. ET to clarify that Abhishek Raval is an analyst at AlphaValue. An earlier article misstated the firm's name.

1110 GMT - Sales of Meta Platforms' smartglasses are going to continue to accelerate, and this will also benefit fourth-quarter revenue for its partner, Ray-Ban maker EssilorLuxottica, RBC Capital Markets analyst Piral Dadhania says. Meta released a new pair of smartglasses with artificial intelligence on Wednesday--a newer version of its Ray-Ban smartglasses and athlete-specific smartglasses with Oakley, which is also owned by EssilorLuxottica. The decision to build out a smartglasses product range across different prices is positive, Dadhania says. The smartglasses sell for between $379 to $799. Shares in Meta are up 0.7% in U.S. premarket trading, and Paris-listed shares in EssilorLuxottica are up 1.6%. (aimee.look@wsj.com)

data.title: Financial Services Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Financial Services. Exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0807 GMT - Asian economies and assets are likely to benefit from the Fed's decision to ease earlier this week, say economists and strategists at Manulife Investment Management. The Fed's move is expected to bring positive momentum for emerging markets, while the resumption of its easing cycle could loosen financial conditions, boost investor sentiment, which bodes well for risk assets. Asia can attract continued capital inflows as the Fed's cut supports risk appetite. "With lower inflationary pressures and a weaker U.S. dollar, EM Asia currencies have the scope to catch up in an environment where volatility remains low, especially for high yielders in the region," they say.(amanda.lee@wsj.com)

0506 GMT - The Bank of Japan's planned sale of domestic equity exchange-traded funds may be insignificant to the market in terms of its pace of selling. The BOJ says it intends to sell ETFs worth about 620 billion yen in market value annually, which is the same pace it had been selling its domestic stock holdings until July this year. Despite the BOJ's stock sales and policy-rate increases, the Japanese stock market has maintained its rising trend in recent years to hit record highs. The central bank purchased domestic stocks from banks in the early 2000s and in the wake of the 2008 global financial crisis as part of extraordinary measures to support the financial system. (kosaku.narioka@wsj.com; @kosakunarioka)

2311 GMT [Dow Jones]--Morgan Stanley estimates ANZ needs to find up to A$2.0 billion of cost savings if it wants to achieve likely medium-term targets under new CEO Nuno Matos. Analyst Richard E. Wiles thinks twin goals of 11-12% return on equity and a CTI ratio of less than 48% within three years would be plausible. MS says its forecasts already assume cost savings totaling A$1.15 billion, including from changes to simplify the bank. "We estimate that even greater savings of A$1.5 billion-A$2.0 billion would require cost reduction of 15-20% in Australian banking and the group center, with a reduction of 10.0-12.5% in the Institutional and New Zealand divisions," MS says. Its price target rises 2.7% to A$30.50/share. ANZ ended Thursday at A$32.86. (david.winning@wsj.com)

1156 GMT - The earnings drivers behind Allianz's growth are stronger than initially expected and are set to continue delivering well beyond the end of its current plan in 2027, Berenberg says. The German insurance giant's three key drivers are its target to reduce its non-life cost ratio by 30 basis points per year, the increase in operating capital generation on more efficient capital allocation at country level and self-funding growth in its life-insurance division. The third-quarter outlook is strong, analyst Michael Huttner writes. "We believe that net inflows at PIMCO remain strong, that pricing in motor in France and Germany is continuing to improve...and that there are continuing high margins in Italian non-life," he notes, adding this should more than offset the effect of a weaker U.S. dollar. Shares are up 0.35% at 345.7 euros. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

1142 GMT - Merger and acquisition activity in the European banking sector is set to continue, according to a new report by law firm White & Case. This will be driven by what the report describes as an ideal alignment with banks returning to non-government hands, the reduction of bad-loan portfolios, better capitalizations and a period of high interest rates having filled up their M&A war chests. Challenger bank consolidation will also continue given the sharper focus on profitability and appetite among private-equity owners for exits, it notes. Germany and Poland are expected to join Italy and Spain in the spotlight. "The floodgates of European bank public M&A are open...H2 2025 and H1 2026 will be anything but dull," partner Hyder Jumabhoy says. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

data.title: Energy & Utilities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Energy and Utilities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0612 GMT - Electricity Generating's acquisition of 30% stake in Indonesia's Chandra Daya Investasi unlocks value, CGS International's Rasmiman Sermprasert says in a research report. This stake is expected to contribute at least 11% of the Thai company's total profit per year over 2026-2027, the analyst estimates. Hence, the brokerage lifts its 2026 and 2027 core net profit forecasts for Electricity Generating by 57% and 37%, respectively, reflecting the earnings accretion from the Indonesian company, which is now a publicly listed entity in Indonesia. The brokerage raises the stock's rating to add from hold and the target price to THB130.00 from THB110.00. Shares are 0.4% higher at THB116.00. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

2322 GMT [Dow Jones]--Optimists on Santos will pivot to a "break up" argument in the aftermath of the XRG-led consortium dropping its $18.7 billion takeover offer, Morgans says. For those investors, the sum of Santos's parts are worth more than its whole and separate domestic natural-gas and LNG businesses would support higher valuations. "Certainly possible, but given the reserve constraints faced by both GLNG and Darwin LNG we do not treat this as a probable scenario to consider," analyst Adrian Prendergast says, referring to Santos's LNG facilities in Australia. Morgans cuts Santos to trim, from accumulate, saying the stock looks modestly overvalued on fundamentals alone. (david.winning@wsj.com)

2258 GMT [Dow Jones]--Santos's share price is likely to remain volatile in the near term as investors adjust to the Australian energy company no longer being a bid target of the XRG consortium, Morgan Stanley says. Investors in Santos must digest several uncertainties, including around domestic energy supply and the decommissioning of some assets. It will take time for fundamentals, such as Santos's free cash flow yield, to reassert themselves, analyst Rob Koh says. MS cuts its price target on Santos by 21% to A$7.00/share, but retains an equal-weight call. Karoon Energy presents the most upside for investors among Australian energy stocks, MS says. Santos ended Thursday at A$6.74, down 12% across that session after the XRG consortium withdraw its bid proposal. (david.winning@wsj.com)

1921 GMT - Oil futures add to yesterday's losses as the high-demand summer season begins to wind down and the market waits to see what if any additional sanctions are placed on Russian oil. The big decline in U.S. crude stocks reported by the EIA is attributed mostly to the jump in exports. "While the drawdown in crude inventories was impressive, it seems to be mostly export driven with the help of near multi-year lows in the U.S. dollar, and not necessarily from domestic demand," Dennis Kissler of BOK Financial says in a note. WTI settles down 0.7% at $63.57 a barrel, which Kissler sees as the near-term support. Brent falls 0.8% to $67.44 a barrel. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1730 GMT - Crude futures are losing ground for a second straight session as the market turns attention back to supply and demand. "People are talking about too much supply chasing too little demand. There is demand out there, but it has not been growing at the same speed" as supply, Carole Nakhle, CEO of Crystol Energy says in a Welligence Energy Analytics webcast. "But prices have not crashed, which means that we are still able to absorb the additional barrels from OPEC+ and elsewhere." Economic data don't translate into strong demand growth, but China is still buying oil for storage. "It raises the question of how much further they can go accumulating all those barrels and for how long," Nakhle added. WTI is down 0.9% at $63.46 a barrel and Brent is 0.9% lower at $67.35. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1328 GMT - Oil futures are flitting between small gains and losses as the market waits to see what additional sanctions the EU will impose against Russia. The possibility of disruptions in Russian oil supply has supported prices the past week, while concerns about higher OPEC+ output keeps a lid on gains. This week's large U.S. crude inventory draw and the Fed's interest rate cut "have helped to keep the losses in check," ING strategists Ewa Manthey and Warren Patterson say in a note. WTI is flat at $64.05 a barrel and Brent edges up 0.1% to $68.01. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

data.title: Basic Materials Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Basic Materials. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0801 GMT - London-listed miners gain in early morning European trading as gold ticks higher. Gold futures are up 0.2% at $3,685.20 a troy ounce and nearly 10% higher this month on U.S. interest rate cut hopes and an uncertain geopolitical backdrop. Endeavour Mining and Fresnillo are leading the FTSE 100 index risers, up 3% and 2.5%, respectively. Anglo American shares are up 1.3%, while Antofagasta shares are trading 1% higher (aimee.look@wsj.com)

0638 GMT - Comex gold futures have likely shifted into a phase of consolidation, based on the daily chart, RHB Retail Research's Joseph Chai says in a report. The futures' bearish price action on Thursday confirms strong resistance has formed at $3,700/oz, the analyst says. The commodity's bullish momentum has decelerated, as indicated by the relative strength index, Chai says. Hence, futures could pull back toward the up-trending 20-day simple moving average, the analyst adds. Spot gold is 0.1% higher at $3,646.34/oz.(ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

2334 GMT - Pantoro Gold's stock has more than tripled in value since the start of the year, supported by prices of the precious metal repeatedly hitting new record highs. "Pantoro appears to have turned a corner operationally and we see upside to FY 2026 guidance given recent performance," analyst Ross Bennett says. "However, the recent stock-price rally has somewhat diminished short-term upside." Morgans starts Pantoro at "hold," with a A$5.33/share price target. It expects current commodity prices to help drive solid earnings and production growth for Pantoro. "These are complemented by >50% Ebitda margin in our base case," Morgans adds. Pantoro ended Thursday at A$5.35. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

1844 GMT - Gold futures fall further, with investors having the benefit of Fed Chair Powell's press conference following the central bank's 25 basis point rate cut Wednesday. "I don't think metals liked Powell's comment regarding his hardline stance against the idea of a 50 [basis-point] cut," says John Caruso of RJO Futures in a note. Caruso adds that longer-term, a Fed program with several rate cuts ahead is supportive for gold prices. Front-month gold futures finish down 1% to $3,643.70 a troy ounce, marking the second consecutive day that front-month gold settled lower. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1512 GMT - BASF's 10-billion-euro investment in a new China plant has put considerable strain on its balance sheet, compromising the German chemical giant's dividend, BofA Securities analyst Matthew Yates says in a note. "We question the contribution BASF's new China plant will make to profits as it ramps up amidst depressed market conditions," he says. According to Yates, the plant also risks cannibalizing the European footprint due to shifting trade flows. This could lead to the need for more restructuring, "which BASF appears reluctant to contemplate, or can ill afford to do." BofA downgrades BASF to underperform from neutral. Shares trade 1.2% lower at 42.97 euros. (sarah.sloat@wsj.com)

1315 GMT - Gold futures are backing away from a recent streak of record-high closes, with the most-active contract down 0.6%. But in the aftermath of the Fed's 25 basis point rate cut, many in the gold market think factors remain in place to push gold to new heights. "When rates are lower, we see yields drop and cash is less attractive, fueling gold demand," says Joseph Cavatoni of the World Gold Council. Cavatoni says that the WGC expects more demand for gold from western investors, although in the short-term volatility is expected--with some traders opting to lock in profits. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

data.title: Auto & Transport Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering the Auto and Transport sector. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0753 GMT - Automakers likely face persistent volatility in the near term, but third-quarter sales started relatively well in Europe and the U.S., Berenberg analysts write. Strong product-launch momentum in the second half and into 2026 and 2027 should also provide some support amid continuing pricing pressure, they add. Headwinds from existing 27.5% U.S. tariffs on European vehicles will continue weighing on margins until the 15% tariffs come into effect. A further softening in China will pressure near-term estimates. Berenberg's top picks are BMW, Ferrari and Renault. BMW remains fundamentally strong, there is upside to Ferrari consensus earnings in 2026-27 and Renault valuation remains attractive, it adds. It upgrades Stellantis stock to buy from hold and lifts its price target to 9.50 euros from 9 euros. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0606 GMT - ComfortDelGro is expected to deliver near-term mid-teens net profit growth, resilient cash flows and a 6.3% dividend yield in 2026, says RHB analyst Shekhar Jaiswal. The bank highlights improving UK public transport margins, capacity for new mergers and acquisitions, and stable point-to-point revenue from taxis and private hires in Singapore as share price drivers. The Singapore-based transport operator has also pre-qualified for the contract to operate the Copenhagen Metro. While the short-term earnings impact may be modest, a successful bid would mark its strategic entry into continental Europe, enhance its profile for future global tenders and diversify earnings opportunities beyond Singapore, the UK, and Australia, Jaiswal adds. RHB maintains its buy rating on ComfortDelGro with a S$1.75 target price. Shares were recently at S$1.49. (jason.chau@wsj.com)

2327 GMT [Dow Jones]--FedEx doesn't believe its sales growth in the fiscal first quarter were the result of consumers trying to pull ahead of tariffs, Chief Customer Officer Brie Carere says during a call with analysts. The shipping company did get a boost in July from Amazon Prime week, which helped the company log a 3% increase in total revenue for the quarter, Carere says. But American consumers appear resilient despite trade uncertainty, which Carere sees as an indicator that their demand for shipping will continue beyond the first quarter. "We saw strong volumes in July, but I don't necessarily see that as a pull forward," Carere says. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

1635 GMT - More vessels loaded with grain are leaving the U.S. Gulf, according to data from the USDA. In its latest Grain Transportation Report, the agency says that an average 29 vessels were loaded with grain at a U.S. Gulf port per week over the past four weeks, versus an average of 24 vessels per week in the prior four weeks. Higher demand for U.S. corn exports is one explanation, the USDA says, reflecting what the agency says is "U.S. export competitiveness and robust early-season demand." Low prices per bushel and a weaker U.S. dollar are helping propel demand. Most-active CBOT corn futures are down 0.6%, while soybeans sink 0.7% and wheat falls 0.6%. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1426 GMT - Spirit Airlines is leaning on its pilots to find a way to save $100 million a year, according to a message from the pilots' union to members. The union told pilots that Spirit's financial situation has reached a "critical point" and change is coming, whether through a negotiated agreement, a judge's order, or Spirit's liquidation. The company has told the pilots that if there's no agreement by Oct. 1, it intends to seek relief from the court, where the pilots' contract could be rejected. (alison.sider@wsj.com; @alyrose)

1422 GMT - Spirit Airlines is contracting. The airline, which last month filed for bankruptcy less than a year after emerging from a previous bankruptcy, plans to shrink its schedule by 25% from a year ago to focus on its strongest markets, CEO Dave Davis tells employees in a memo. Spirit's first chapter 11 failed to address all of the airlines' financial issues, including costly airplane leases. Now the carrier is evaluating how many planes it will need for its smaller footprint and plans to meet with unions to discuss cost-cutting measures, Davis says. "These evaluations will inevitably affect the size of our teams as we become a more efficient airline." (alison.sider@wsj.com; @alyrose)

1055 GMT - Grab is expected to strengthen its market leadership, HSBC analysts say in a note. The bank forecasts the ride-hailing and delivery platform's on-demand gross merchandise value to grow 19% in 2025 and at a compound annual growth rate of 17.2% over 2024-2027. "We expect margins to improve with increased contributions from high-value offerings, scale and advertising revenue," HSBC says. However, HSBC downgrades Grab to hold from buy following the stock's 77% gain over the past 12 months and on its view that the risk-reward is balanced. The bank raises its target price to $6.20 from $6.00 on likely market-share gains in the on-demand segment. Grab last closed at $6.10. (jason.chau@wsj.com)

0838 GMT - Bollore's new investment opportunities after the Vivendi split and solid first-half profitability ease some concerns over its legal risks, Baader analysts write. The French conglomerate posted mixed first-half earnings Tuesday following the deconsolidation of its Vivendi holding, but saw strong profitability thanks to the inclusion of resulting spinoffs. Bollore also reduced its Universal Music Group stake, which may be excessive, the analysts say. It could deploy those shares in a Vivendi takeover, Baader says. Still, litigation risks remain following fraud complaints over port concessions made in March by groups from five African countries. Shares are down 0.7% at 4.8 euros. (william.gray@wsj.com)

Russian delegation discusses prospects for trade and economic cooperation, including in gas, with Iranian leadership
data.description:
A Russian delegation has discussed prospects for trade and economic cooperation with the Iranian leadership, including in the gas sector, during a visit to Tehran, as per Interfax.

Russian Energy Minister Sergei Tsivilev and the CEO of PJSC Gazprom Alexei Miller held working meetings with Iranian President Masoud Pezeshkian and Oil Minister Mohsen Paknejad. Representatives of relevant agencies and companies from both countries participated in the events.

"The parties discussed prospects for trade and economic cooperation, including in the gas sector," Gazprom said.The Russian Energy Ministry said that the progress in implementing agreements reached following the 18th meeting of the intergovernmental commission (IGC), which took place on April 23-25, 2025, in Moscow, was also discussed. Particular attention was paid to preparations for the 19th meeting of the IGC, which will be held in Iran. Tsivilev and Paknejad co-chair the bilateral IGC.A meeting was also held with the head of the National Iranian Gas Company, Seyed Tavakoli. A strategic memorandum was signed in 2024 on exploring the organization of supplies of Russian natural gas to Iran, Gazprom said.Following the April IGC meeting, Tsivilev said that supplies to Iran could begin as early as 2025. At the first stage, using existing infrastructure, they could reach up to 1.8 billion cubic meters per year. The potential for supplies is estimated at 55 bcm per year.
